INTC.title: Trump Administration's Intel Stake Is Now Up 290% In Less Than A Year

macro_events.event_summary: Dow CEO warns the shipping logjam in the Strait of Hormuz will take nearly a year to clear, implying prolonged disruption to Middle East energy and petrochemical flows.
primary_drivers: Prolonged supply disruption to Middle East crude/LNG/petrochemical exports lifting global energy prices
Tanker/shipping rate spike from rerouting and vessel scarcity
Input cost inflation and feedstock scarcity for global chemical producers reliant on Gulf ethane/naphtha

beneficiaries.rationale: Frontline is a pure-play crude tanker operator; a year-long Hormuz logjam forces rerouting around Africa, extending ton-miles and tightening VLCC/Suezmax supply — directly lifts spot tanker day rates.
International Seaways operates VLCCs and product tankers; prolonged Gulf disruption increases product tanker demand as refined product trade patterns rebalance globally.

Scorpio Tankers is the largest product tanker fleet; Hormuz disruption of Gulf refined product exports (Saudi, UAE, Kuwait refineries) forces longer-haul replacements from US/Europe/Asia, lifting MR/LR rates.

Exxon has large US Gulf Coast and Permian production/refining exposure that benefits from higher Brent and widening light-heavy spreads as Middle East barrels get stranded; also benefits from higher downstream chemical margins as Gulf petchem competitors are sidelined.

Cheniere is the largest US LNG exporter; Qatar LNG (~20% of global supply) routes through Hormuz — a year of disruption structurally redirects Asian/European buyers to US Gulf Coast cargoes, supporting utilization and spot margins.

LyondellBasell is a major US ethylene/polyolefins producer using advantaged US ethane; Gulf petchem capacity (SABIC, Borouge, Sadara — Dow's JV) being constrained for a year directly tightens global polyethylene/polypropylene supply and lifts US producer margins.
Westlake is a US-based PVC/chlorovinyls and polyethylene producer; benefits from the same global petchem supply tightness as Middle East volumes are trapped behind the logjam.

losers.rationale: Dow's own CEO flagging the disruption — Dow has the Sadara JV in Saudi Arabia and significant Gulf feedstock/logistics exposure; a year-long logjam impairs JV output, working capital, and supply commitments. Guidance risk into next print.
Delta has no fuel hedges and high international exposure; sustained Brent premium from Hormuz disruption directly pressures unit fuel cost and margins.
United has the largest transpacific/international network among US carriers; prolonged jet fuel cost elevation from Middle East disruption hits CASM-ex through 2026.
Carnival's bunker fuel is a major variable cost; a year of elevated crude from Hormuz disruption directly compresses margins, and Middle East/Asia itineraries may be rerouted.

Walmart faces import cost pressure as Asia-Europe-US container rates rise on rerouting spillover and as plastic packaging/resin costs (tied to petchem tightness) climb — squeezes gross margin into back half.

Celanese relies on global acetyl and engineered materials supply chains with Middle East/Asia nodes; feedstock dislocation and trapped inventory create working capital and volume headwinds despite price benefits.

headlines: Dow CEO says clearing the Strait of Hormuz logjam will take almost a year
